rationales.self_direction: She says 'My hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise,' showing her own artistic judgment being overruled.
rationales.conformity: She accepts that 'you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive,' prioritizing fitting the committee's expectations.
rationales.universalism: Her sketch keeps returning to 'the river contamination,' showing persistent concern with environmental harm rather than a purely decorative mural.

rationales.self_direction: She envies David's 'freedom' and says she learned 'all the reasons to hold back,' describing a pattern of restraining her own voice.
rationales.universalism: What moves her is David's collage about 'flooding' with 'garbage and river water mixed together,' an unsanitized response to ecological damage.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to a project with 'No committee, no brief, no polite rewrites' and wants to 'paint what you see and feel,' embracing independent expression.
rationales.universalism: She joins a march about 'deforestation in Guaviare,' studies satellite images of the cleared land, and worries whether the family's farm still exists.

rationales.self_direction: She 'compromised' and softened the contamination imagery, then describes a portfolio full of 'images I softened' and 'opinions I kept,' showing repeated self-suppression.
rationales.conformity: She frames public art as serving 'the community's vision' and keeps her sharper views private so she can stay careful with the committee and family.
rationales.universalism: Even in the softened mural she is still trying to signal 'that something is wrong underneath,' keeping attention on environmental damage.

rationales.self_direction: She typed a response 'three times' and deleted all of them, then sent only a 'sad emoji,' acting against her own urge to speak plainly.
rationales.conformity: She stays quiet because she can already picture 'the awkward silence at Easter dinner,' choosing family harmony over open disagreement.
rationales.universalism: She wants to name 'the logging companies,' the 'lack of enforcement,' and families 'being displaced,' showing clear concern for broader social and environmental harm.

rationales.self_direction: She says the mural feels like 'the paint is saying exactly what I mean,' which is a direct return to making work on her own terms.
rationales.universalism: This is the 'march mural,' and calling it 'honest work' shows she is fully expressing the environmental cause behind it.

rationales.conformity: She tells her mother it is 'just a painting, nothing political,' smoothing over the truth to avoid upsetting her.
rationales.benevolence: She says 'she doesn't need to carry that. She has enough,' showing she is protecting her mother from extra worry.

rationales.self_direction: She had already said yes to speaking and tells herself 'this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine,' moving beyond hiding behind the mural.
rationales.conformity: She is preparing to put 'My name attached to words' even though family voices keep saying 'be careful,' which goes against staying socially safe and unobtrusive.
rationales.universalism: The finished mural shows 'the forest canopy dissolving into ash' and families walking between 'green and grey,' making the environmental loss central.

rationales.self_direction: She actually spoke at the march despite fear, then notes she is 'glad I did it once,' showing she acted on her own convictions.
rationales.conformity: She went forward even after imagining 'my mother crying' and 'my uncle calling me irresponsible,' accepting the risk of family disapproval.
rationales.universalism: Her speech focused on 'a place disappearing' and 'someone losing their home,' tying her public stance to environmental and human harm.

rationales.conformity: The 'loudest' voice tells her to 'just be grateful someone asked,' 'don't be difficult,' and work 'within the lines,' showing a strong pull toward staying agreeable and compliant.

rationales.achievement: She spent 'three months' on the protocol 'on my own time' because she wanted to 'prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care,' and the senior doctor's praise clearly matters to her.
She picks up 'two extra shifts' because 'being reliable matters when you're trying to move up,' tying the extra work to advancement.
rationales.benevolence: She covered for Anja and talked the new grad through a first code 'step by step,' giving close, practical support under pressure.

rationales.conformity: When Klara asked about losing the charge role, she said 'fine' and changed the subject, managing the interaction instead of voicing the disappointment.

rationales.achievement: She keeps refreshing the posting for 'the junior ward manager' role, showing strong focus on career progress and external advancement.
She says falling behind would make her 'invisible' and insists 'I can't just be average,' revealing a strong drive to stand out through work.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps checking work emails 'even when I'm home' after Thomas says he never sees her, letting career demands crowd out attention to a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She cannot remember 'the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift' or 'the last time I left on time,' sacrificing basic comfort and rest for work.
rationales.achievement: Even after a 6am to midnight shift, she frames the strain around the review and says what would make it worth it is 'The title. The recognition.'
She says yes because 'It's exposure' and 'a chance to show the department head what I can do,' making the decision about proving herself.
rationales.conformity: 'What else am I going to say?' and 'This is how it works' show her accepting the workplace script even though it costs her.
rationales.benevolence: She knowingly takes on more work 'even if Thomas will eat dinner alone again,' putting advancement ahead of her partner.
She knows she could 'probably make it work' for Thomas's cousin's wedding but still avoids committing, withholding support for someone close to her.

rationales.achievement: She treats the praise from 'all four new hires' as a 'Small win,' showing that positive feedback is meaningful evidence of doing well.
rationales.benevolence: A new hire said she made the 'defibrillator protocols actually make sense,' showing she helped others handle critical work more confidently.
She forgot 'our anniversary' and dismissed the phone reminder during a shift, neglecting an important commitment to her partner.

rationales.achievement: She spends the evening updating a self-assessment with 'all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work,' clearly oriented toward proving merit in the review.
